name: ilex vomitoria
mechanism: Likely stimulant effects are primarily caffeine-mediated adenosine receptor antagonism; phytochemical diversity (including chlorogenic-acid-type phenolics) may contribute antioxidant-related properties, but clinical outcome evidence is sparse.
effects: No direct effects data. Contextual inference:
nan.. No direct mechanismofaction data. Contextual inference:
safetyNotes: Safety profile is expected to track caffeine exposure: possible insomnia, palpitations, anxiety, reflux, or tremor with higher intake. Use caution in pregnancy, arrhythmia risk, and alongside other stimulant sources.

description: Yaupon is an evergreen holly native to the southeastern United States and nearby regions. Leaves are roasted/toasted and infused as a tea-like beverage.
dosage: No established therapeutic dosing standard. Intake is usually beverage-based and should be titrated by caffeine tolerance and total daily stimulant burden.

interactions: Additive stimulant/cardiovascular effects with caffeine-containing products or sympathomimetics.
preparation: Typically prepared as roasted or unroasted leaf infusion (yaupon tea); commercial products include loose-leaf and bagged teas.
region: Native to the southeastern United States and adjoining regions of North America.
therapeuticUses: Native American ceremonial drink for stimulation and purification
summary: Ilex vomitoria (yaupon) is a North American holly traditionally brewed as a caffeinated beverage. Modern analyses confirm methylxanthines (notably caffeine) and polyphenolic constituents, while direct clinical trial data remain limited.
